title: Measuring your household carbon footprint
subtitle: Calculating operational carbon from energy bills and travel data
excerpt: A UK household generates approximately 8-12 tonnes of CO2 equivalent per year from all sources. The home heating and electricity contribution is typically 2-4 tonnes. Measuring it accurately using actual energy data identifies where reductions have the most impact.
sourceNotes: DESNZ Greenhouse Gas Conversion Factors for Company Reporting (2024 edition). Energy Saving Trust household carbon footprint methodology. Carbon Trust consumer footprinting guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: CO2 equivalent emissions from running a building: heating (gas, oil, biomass), electricity, and hot water. Does not include embodied carbon of construction. The operational carbon of UK homes accounts for approximately 14% of total UK emissions.

CO2 emissions from producing and delivering products and materials consumed by the household. For most UK households, food accounts for the largest share of embodied carbon (approximately 25% of total footprint); manufactured goods and home renovation materials account for much of the rest.

The gCO2e per kWh of grid electricity. The annual average UK grid carbon intensity is approximately 207 gCO2e per kWh (2024 DESNZ figure). This is the figure used for annual household footprinting calculations for electricity.

Kilowatt-hour: the unit of energy. Annual gas consumption for a 3-bedroom UK semi is approximately 11,500 kWh; electricity approximately 3,500 kWh. These figures appear on energy bills as the basis for carbon calculation.

The total carbon reduction over the life of an improvement, calculated as (annual carbon saving) x (years of operation). A loft insulation top-up saving 600 kWh of gas per year saves approximately 109 kg CO2 per year and approximately 1635 kg over a 15-year period.
For carbon accounting: the time for a carbon investment (embodied carbon of the improvement) to be repaid by the operational carbon saving. A measure of the net carbon benefit of an intervention.

content.text: The home energy contribution to a household's
is calculable from annual energy bills. Annual gas consumption (in

) multiplied by 0.182 kgCO2e/kWh (DESNZ 2024 natural gas factor) gives the gas contribution in kg CO2e. Annual electricity consumption multiplied by 0.207 kgCO2e/kWh (DESNZ 2024 UK grid average) gives the electricity contribution.
A 3-bedroom semi-detached house in the UK: gas 12,000 kWh/year, electricity 3,500 kWh/year.
Gas: 12,000 x 0.182 = 2184 kg CO2e per year (2.18 tonnes)
Electricity: 3,500 x 0.207 = 725 kg CO2e per year (0.72 tonnes) at UK average
Total home energy: approximately 2.9 tonnes CO2e per year

Your energy supplier provides annual kWh consumption on your bill or online account. If you have a smart meter, you can see daily and hourly consumption data. If you do not have smart metering, read your meter at the same time on two days exactly one week apart, multiply by 52 for an annual figure. An energy monitor clamp measures electricity consumption in real-time without requiring a smart meter.

Once you have a baseline, each improvement can be evaluated for its carbon reduction. Loft insulation top-up (100 mm to 270 mm) typically saves 600 to 900 kWh of gas per year: 109 to 164 kg CO2e per year. A smart thermostat saves 10 to 15% of heating energy: 200 to 330 kg CO2e per year in this example household. A heat pump replacing the gas boiler, at SCOP 3 with the current grid factor, saves approximately 1700 kg CO2e per year.

(embodied carbon of the measure divided by annual saving) puts improvements in priority order. Insulation and draught-proofing typically have very short carbon paybacks (under 2 years); large system replacements have longer paybacks but larger absolute reductions.

attrs.title: The full footprint picture
attrs.body: Home energy typically accounts for 25-35% of a UK household's total carbon footprint. Flights (if taken), car use, and food account for most of the rest. A household that flies long-haul twice a year adds approximately 4-8 tonnes of CO2e from flights alone. The home energy calculation is the starting point for identifying where effort has the most impact, not the whole picture.

content.text: of household consumption (goods, food, services) typically exceeds the operational carbon of the home for UK households. Both matter; the home energy calculation gives the part over which direct technical improvements apply.
